Search results: 



--- SOURCE 1: The Omics Revolution in Agricultural Research - PMC ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4714296/

SUMMARY:
Omics has provided insights to the molecular mechanisms of insect resistance to pesticides, and the tolerance of plants to herbicides for better pest management. Linking genes to traits provides more scientific certainty leading to improved cultivars and understanding the mechanisms of insect and weed resistance .



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Omics: A new, promising technologies for boosting crop yield and ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204

SUMMARY:
Omics technologies (OTs), which include genomics, proteomics, and metabolomics, have become a game-changing strategy for enhancing food crops in Africa (Benkeblia,2014; Nayak et al., 2021). Therefore, Africa has the potential to overcome agricultural obstacles and enhance food security by utilizing the power of OTs by creating crop varieties with higher yields, better nutritional profiles, and increased climate change resilience (Muthamilarasan et al., 2019). For Africa to experience improved food security, crop yields must be increased as well as stress resilience (Bjornlund et al., 2022). African crop varieties with improved yields have benefited from the development of OTs. Using genomics, scientists have created disease-resistant varieties, improved drought tolerance (Singh et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2021), and boosted nutritional traits in crops (Anil Kumar et al., 2016; Rana et al., 2020).

FULL CONTENT:
Omics: A new, promising technologies for boosting crop yield and stress resilience in African agriculture - ScienceDirect

Review

Omics: A new, promising technologies for boosting crop yield and stress resilience in African agriculture[☆](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#aep-article-footnote-id1)
==================================================================================================================================================================================================================

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •[Agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about Agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in Africa is hampered by some problems, such as poor infrastructure, pests, diseases, and the effects of [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as droughts and unpredictable rainfall patterns. Innovative strategies that can increase the productivity and toughness of African food crops are required to address these problems. 
*   •According to numerous studies ([Scossa et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0144); [Yang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0176); [Ali et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0006)), OTs, such as genomics, [proteomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/proteomics "Learn more about proteomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [metabolomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/metabolomics "Learn more about metabolomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), have a significant capacity for crop enhancement. 
*   •However, its application to enhancing food crops in Africa remains somewhat limited. There is a lack of knowledge regarding how to fully utilize OTs to tackle the unique difficulties faced by African agriculture. 
*   •The focus of this review is on the most recent research on using OTs to enhance African crops. The use of OTs to raise the productivity and nutritional value of agricultural food crops is covered in this article. 
*   •The knowledge provided here will help with the development of crop breeding innovations that increase yields and efficiency for a variety of African crops. 

Abstract
--------

African agricultural systems are now in crucial need to assure food for the overgrowing population. To meet this challenges, we need better way to identify and modify key proteins and genes involved in yield potential, nutrient value, and stress tolerance in crucial African crops like maize, sorghum, soybeans, cassava, rice, etc. The deep understanding of [metabolomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/metabolomics "Learn more about metabolomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), transcriptomics, [proteomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/proteomics "Learn more about proteomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and genomics has proven to be a useful tool for increasing crop yield and stress resilience in Africa. However, there is very little information available regarding the recent use of omics technology to increase crop yields in Africa. This review provided the recent advances in plants omics and molecular profiling techniques to enhance our understanding of crop breeding strategies for crops grown in Africa. This study will enhance our understanding of plant omics technologies and inform crop breeding strategies.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000162)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000101)

Keywords
--------

Omics

Transcriptomics

Genomics

Proteomics

Metabolomics

Agriculture

Africa

1. Introduction
---------------

Omics technologies (OTs), which include genomics, [proteomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/proteomics "Learn more about proteomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [metabolomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/metabolomics "Learn more about metabolomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), have become a game-changing strategy for enhancing food crops in Africa ([Benkeblia,2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0016); [Nayak et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0113)). [Agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about Agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in Africa is hampered by some problems, such as poor infrastructure, pests, diseases, and the effects of [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as droughts and unpredictable rainfall patterns ([Gbashi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0048); [Kavhiza et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0075)). The study of an organism's entire gene pool, or genomics, allows scientists to pinpoint the genes responsible for desirable characteristics like yield, disease resilience, and [calories](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/calorie "Learn more about calories from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). To create crop varieties with enhanced characteristics, breeding programs can make use of this knowledge. The primary goal of [proteomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/proteomics "Learn more about proteomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is to understand how proteins are expressed and function in plants, as well as how they react to various stresses and possible [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") targets. A crop's entire set of metabolites is examined by [metabolomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/metabolomics "Learn more about metabolomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which helps to comprehend metabolic pathways and pinpoint chemicals that are associated with crop performance and [stress tolerance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/stress "Learn more about stress tolerance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Mathur et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0097); [Ramalingam et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0132)). OTs enable high-throughput screening and analysis, allowing researchers to find genetic and molecular markers connected to significant traits ([Alorda-Clara et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0007); [Vailati-Riboni et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0164)). OTs aid in the responsible creation and analysis of genetically modified (GM) crops by contributing to the evaluation of safety, usability, and environmental impact ([Benevenuto et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0015); [Davies,2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0034); [Gong and Wang,2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0053)). This knowledge is essential for creating GM crop varieties that are resistant to the biotic and [abiotic stresses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/abiotic-stress "Learn more about abiotic stresses from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that are common in Africa. Mmbando et al., for instance, used OTs to create [transgenic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transgenics "Learn more about transgenic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") African rice _[Oryza glaberrima](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/oryza-glaberrima "Learn more about Oryza glaberrima from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ cultivars with improved ultraviolet-B (UVB) resistance mechanisms ([Mmbando et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0103)). Furthermore, having access to omics data can encourage researcher collaboration and data sharing, leading to the creation of extensive databases and platforms for crop enhancement ([Schumacher et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0143)). Therefore, Africa has the potential to overcome agricultural obstacles and enhance food security by utilizing the power of OTs by creating crop varieties with higher yields, better nutritional profiles, and increased climate change resilience ([Muthamilarasan et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0107)).

African crops benefit greatly from the comprehensive understanding of [crop genetics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/crop-genetics "Learn more about crop genetics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), molecular processes, and responses to environmental factors provided by genomics, proteomics, [transcriptomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/transcriptomics "Learn more about transcriptomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and metabolomics ([Ghazal et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0051); [Mukankusi et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0105); [Quansah and Karikari,2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0130)). For instance, genomics aids in determining the genes in charge of important features. Genetic research on African maize has identified genes associated with [drought tolerance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/drought-tolerance "Learn more about drought tolerance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which has made it possible to create the Water Efficient Maize for Africa (WEMA) and Drought Tolerant Maize for Africa (DTMA) projects ([Nasser et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0112); [Oikeh et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0119)). Furthermore, genomic information is essential for maintaining the [genetic diversity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetic-divergence "Learn more about genetic diversity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of African crops, including rice ([Ndjiondjop et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0114)). By conserving the genetic resources of underutilized crops through genomics, the African Orphan Crops Consortium is able to protect biodiversity and maximize crop production potential ([Ghazal et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0051); [Hendre et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0064); [Yaqoob et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0177)). Given that pearl [millet](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/panicum-miliaceum "Learn more about millet from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a staple grain that feeds approximately 90 million people in Asia and Africa, the discovery of the [genetic mechanisms](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetic-mechanism "Learn more about genetic mechanisms from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") underneath the plant's resilience to heat and drought ([Varshney et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0166)) may open the door for the continent to discover tolerance to other cereals. Also, it has been demonstrated that sequencing the genome of the white Guinea yam improves efforts to increase food security and preserve the viability of African agriculture ([Tamiru et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0161)). These studies provide crucial information for enhancing [agronomic traits](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/agronomic-trait "Learn more about agronomic traits from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of these crops under current climatic change condition. Moreover, sub-Saharan Africa's underutilized legumes are currently being enhanced through the use of genomics, which may accelerate the development of enhanced cultivars ([Paliwal et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0122)). Proteomic analyses have revealed the proteins responsible for disease resilience in African bananas, which has led to the development of disease-resistant banana varieties through breeding ([Soares et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0154); [Tripathi et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0163)). Proteomics also has the potential to raise crop quality. Proteomics in African cassava has revealed proteins involved in starch [biosynthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/anabolism "Learn more about biosynthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which has helped produce high-grade cassava products ([Ramulifho and Rey,2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0133); [Vanderschuren et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0165)).

[Transcriptome analyses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gene-expression-profiling "Learn more about Transcriptome analyses from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of African rice have revealed genes that are activated during pathogen infection, which has aided in the creation of disease-resistant rice cultivars ([Lambarey et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0083)). For instance, it has been demonstrated that [transcriptomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transcriptomics "Learn more about transcriptomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") analysis offers vital information about the relationship between sweet potatoes and sweet potato virus diseases (SPVD), which helps to provide useful information for the management of viral infections ([Bednarek et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0013)). Transcriptomics and genomics have also been shown to improve the genetic enhancement of underutilized crops, like [Bambara groundnuts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/vigna-subterranea "Learn more about Bambara groundnuts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which help us understand how legumes respond to stress and how to adapt to drought ([Khan et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0076)). Transcriptomics also sheds light on how crops react to environmental stressors. It has provided insight into the molecular mechanisms underlying [drought tolerance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/drought-tolerance "Learn more about drought tolerance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in African millet, thereby guiding breeding initiatives for resilient millet cultivars (J. [Li et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0086); [Zhang et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0179)). In addition, metabolomics offers information about the kinds and quantities of [small molecules](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/small-molecule "Learn more about small molecules from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in crops ([Nkosi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0116)). Metabolomic analyses of African sweet potatoes have revealed differences in the concentrations of critical nutrients, which has aided in the creation of nutrient-dense sweet potato varieties ([Bednarek et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0013); [Drapal et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0040)).It has also been demonstrated that metabolomics is essential for improving the nutritional value of crops ([Shi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0146)). Metabolomics has revealed compounds associated with iron and zinc content in African beans, which has directed the creation of biofortified bean varieties to address [micronutrient deficiencies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/micronutrient-deficiency "Learn more about micronutrient deficiencies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Popoola et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0126)). There is documentation of certain beneficial traits conferred by certain quantitative trait loci (QTLs) in crops grown in Africa ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#tbl0001)). By working together, these omics techniques enable African scientists to create crops that are resilient, nutrient-dense, and climate-appropriate—thus addressing the continent's problems with food security and nutrition.

Table 1. Various identified QTLs or genes of crops in Africa.

| **QTLs** | **Crops** | **References** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Grain weight 2(_GW2), Grain number 1A_(_GN1A_), and _Grain size 3_(_GS3_) | African rice _Oryza glaberrima_ | ([Takeda and Matsuoka,2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0160)) |
| Grain quality; _hr3, Amy6_, and _Alk6-1_ | Interspecific cross between _Oryza sativa_ and _O. glaberrima_ | ([Aluko et al., 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0008)) |
| Resistance to greeen rice hoppers; _qGRH3, qGRH7, qGRH9, qGRH10_ | _O. glaberrima_ | ([Fujita et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0046)) |
| Pod dehiscence; _Pdt_, seed domancy; _Sdwa_, Gigantism; _Sd100wt_ and _Sdl_, Plant type; _Lfa_ and _Stl_, Earliness; _BRNPP, Fld_, and _Pddm_, Yield potential; _Sdnppd_ | Zombi Pea [_Vigna vexillata_(L.) A. Rich] | ([Amkul et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0009)) |
| Yield; _qgy-_ 3, 5, 7.1 _,_ Ears per plant; _qepp-_ 1, 6, 7, 10, Striga damage; _qsd_-1, 3, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 | Maize | ([Badu-Apraku et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0011)) |
| Cassava Brown Streak Disease (CBSD) root necrosis; _qCBSDRNc14AR_) and Cassava Green Mite (CGM) resistance; _qCGM18AR_ | Cassava | ([Garcia-Oliveira et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0047)) |
| stay-green (STG) 3A, STG 3B -Retain STG and PCD triggering, and STG and N mobilization | Sorghum | ([Mwamahonje et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0109)) |

For Africa to experience improved food security, crop yields must be increased as well as stress resilience ([Bjornlund et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0018)). To create high-yielding and stress-tolerant varieties, targeted breeding and genetic engineering techniques can be used ([Mmbando et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0103)). OTs can offer knowledge about the genetic makeup, [protein expression](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-expression "Learn more about protein expression from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and metabolic processes of crops ([Singh et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0151); [Yang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0176)). This can slow down environmental deterioration and encourage sustainable agricultural intensification in Africa. According to numerous studies (A. [Ali et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0006); [Scossa et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0144); [Yang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0176)), OTs, such as genomics, proteomics, and metabolomics, have a significant capacity for crop enhancement. However, its application to enhancing food crops in Africa remains somewhat limited. There is a lack of knowledge regarding how to fully utilize OTs to tackle the unique difficulties faced by African agriculture.

The focus of this review is on the most recent research on using OTs to enhance African crops. The use of OTs to raise the productivity and nutritional value of agricultural food crops is covered in this article ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#fig0001)). The knowledge provided here will help with the development of crop breeding innovations that increase yields and efficiency for a variety of African crops.

African farmers can improve their [livestock production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/livestock-production "Learn more about livestock production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") quality and food security by utilizing omics technologies like genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics.

2. The current application of omics technology to food crops in Africa
----------------------------------------------------------------------

Applying OTs to African food crops has been the subject of extensive research. The identification of significant genes linked to traits like yield, disease resilience, and nutritional value has been made possible by genomic studies on crucial crops like maize, cassava, and rice ([Damaris et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0031); [Gowda et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0054); [Karmakar et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0074); [Nyaga et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667064X24000204#bib0118); [Scarcell

# Editorial: Omics approaches to improve crops yields

![
Rubab Zahra Naqvi
](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/387466/74)
![Shahid Mansoor](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/287349/74)
![Imran Amin](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/358746/74)

**Editorial on the Research Topic**  
 [**Omics approaches to improve crops yields**](https://www.frontiersin.org/research-topics/53652/omics-approaches-to-improve-crops-yields)

Enhancing crop yield and superior traits is crucial in developing better crop varieties to address climate change and various environmental and biological challenges. Global warming driven by climate change can lead to a rise in insect pests and plant diseases, posing a risk to crop productivity ([Pixley et al., 2023](#B3)). Crop breeding is useful to improve crops yields and favorable traits, nevertheless, the introduction of modern biotechnological procedures and omics disciplines in conventional breeding widens the available tools to speed up the breeding programs ([Naqvi et al., 2022](#B2)). Recent decades have seen significant growth in omics technologies, driven by advancements in next-generation sequencing (NGS), and bioinformatics data analysis algorithms owing to abundance of biological data. The leading omics toolkit include genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics. Several crop species' genomes have been successfully sequenced, and crucial genes linked to economic characteristics are now known. These genes reveal crop trait regulatory mechanisms and provide practical basis for molecular breeding ([Naqvi et al., 2024](#B1)). The focus of the current Research Topic was to illuminate the utilization of latest omics technologies for exploration of crops' genetic insights and identification of more important genes that will be helpful to develop climate-smart crops leading to zero hunger on the planet.

[Zhan et al.](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2022.1070108), investigated the molecular mechanisms triggering the phenotype variations among two diploid and autopolyploid varieties of banana by using transcriptomics. The researchers acquired non-chimeric autotetraploid plants from diploid banana “Pisang Berlin,” that exhibits resistance to Fusarium wilt. The autotetraploids exhibited considerable enhancements in pseudostem diameter, plant height, and root length. Furthermore, genes related to area, thickness, and chlorophyll content in leaves were also upregulated. Transcriptomic analysis identified differential gene expression in hormonal signal transduction, the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signaling, and carbon fixation pathways. Metabolomic study also identified variably accumulating compounds associated with plant hormonal signal transmission, indole alkaloid production, porphyrin and chlorophyll metabolism, and carbon fixation in photosynthetic organelles. The results indicate that the hormones ABA and IAA, along with regulation of photosynthesis, might significantly contribute to the improvement of autotetraploids, potentially facilitating the production of triploid offspring for cultivation.

[Nwachukwu and Babalola](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2022.886987), have reviewed and shed light on the important insights of metagenomics for its exploitation to improve crop plants. Metagenomics is a non-culture approach that captures the complete microbial community in ecological samples, enabling the laboratory analysis of whole genomes devoid of culturing. This non-culture method provides more genetic information than traditional methods. Understanding the interactions between microbes, soil, and plants stays crucial for improving crop yields for viable agriculture. Metagenomic techniques can help develop new bio-pesticides and bio-fertilizers that remain environmentally friendly, thereby improving plant development and sustainable crop production.

Robusta coffee cultivation is crucial due to its higher disease resistance and ability to withstand climate changes. Genetic resources from various gene banks can further improve quality and agronomic traits in coffee. [Verleysen et al.](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1239442), utilized genotyping by sequencing (GBS) to identify genetic diversity in 730 Yangambi Coffee shrubs, identifying 263 unique accessions through a novel HiPlex screening assay. Eight accessions were preferentially used, corresponding to originators. This study described the genetic composition of the INERA Coffee Collection in Yangambi, the Democratic Republic of Congo. The findings unearthed that most accessions dispensed to a cultivar origin 'Lula', while four were assigned to subgroup A of Congolese. The study donates to the re-assessment and evaluation of Robusta coffee germplasm.

Lipidomics and metabolomics are essential tools for investigating crop lipids and metabolites. [Cui et al.](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1119314), reveal dynamical changes in tea lipid and metabolic profiles during manufacture processes. Nonetheless, individual lipidomics or metabolomics cannot discern essential components for quality assessment and authenticity verification. Integrating lipidomics with metabolomics can broaden the breadth of composition analysis, wrapping both polar/non-polar molecules. A technique combining UPLC-Triple-TOF/MS-based lipidomics and metabolomics was established to distinguish early spring green tea (ET) from late spring green tea (LT). This work distinguished the chemical compositions of ET and LT by lipidomics and metabolomics. ET samples contained glycerophospholipids and acylglycerolipids, whereas LT samples comprised glycoglycerolipids, sphingolipids, and hydroxypheophytin. The reconstructed orthogonal partial least squares discriminant analysis (OPLS-DA) model demonstrated a 100% accuracy rate in classifying tea quality based on harvest time.

[Song et al.](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1188159), investigated mepiquat chloride (DPC)'s regulation in soybeans via morphology and physiology, along with proteomic analyses. The study used Heinong44 and Heinong65 soybean varieties, sprayed with DPC solution at the seedling stage. The study used morphological and physiological experiments, tandem mass tag quantitative proteomics, followed by bioinformatics analysis to detect differentially expressed proteins and study changes in the soybean leaf proteome after DPC treatment. Proteomic analysis of 100 mg/L DPC treated leaves showed that numerous proteins associated with plant development and stress tolerance were differentially expressed. Results show that DPC reduces shoot dry weight, plant height and decreases total flavonoids and malondialdehyde. Furthermore, it was found that DPC inhibits shoot growth and photosynthesis but promotes lateral root development, enhances protective enzymes, and improves plant resistance to abiotic stress.

Millets is a promising agricultural crop, that can help ensure global food security due to their resilience against climate change and increasing demand for nutritious food and feed. [Sanku et al.](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2024.1435437), narrated about innovations in NGS technologies like genome-wide expression analysis which can aid in genomic selection by predicting stress tolerance related genes in millet breeding populations. Transcriptomics is an NGS technology that is being utilized to study the impact of differentially expressed genes and transcription factors for millet improvement, potentially revealing breeding opportunities for climate resilience. Advancements in spatial transcriptomics, can further help decode cell transcripts and differentially expressed genes, evading complications instigated by large genomes. This data can be analysed using advanced bioinformatics algorithms to identify significant patterns and regulatory genes. This combined effort can be used in marker assisted selection or genetic transformations for development of crop varieties with climatic resilience. Overall, transcriptomics is essential for improving the resilience of millets and other cereal crops and promoting sustainable agriculture.

Contemporary advancements in NGS have transformed crop breeding and broadened the potential for enhancing agricultural traits. Accordingly, the articles published in this Research Topic portray the prospective of omics approaches for acquiring biological data and digging genes allied with agronomical traits in crop plants. The issue provides valuable information on current research in this area that will provide readers with up-to-date knowledge about mechanisms and genes mined by omics approaches that could be useful for smart breeding programs and forecast essential basis for future crop research.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilizing omics technologies enhances crop yield by identifying key genes linked to yield traits, enabling the development of high-yielding, climate-resilient varieties.
Omics approaches facilitate genetic understanding of stress responses, leading to crops that withstand biotic and abiotic stresses such as drought and pests.
Integrating metabolomics helps in biofortifying crops, enhancing their nutritional profiles to combat micronutrient deficiencies and improve food security.
